region_of_origin: Southeast Asia (widely farmed in Singapore, Australia, Southeast Asia)

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical single serving of raw barramundi fillet is approximately 150 g, equivalent to one medium fillet portion as commonly sold in Singaporean wet markets and supermarkets.

Saturated fat and fatty acid breakdown cross-referenced with USDA FoodData Central entry for raw barramundi (FDC ID 175159) to supplement the HPB record where detailed fatty acid breakdown was not reported.

gi_source.title: N/A — Glycemic index not applicable for negligible-carbohydrate fish
gi_source.source_match_notes: N/A — food contains negligible carbohydrates (0.0 g/100g). Glycemic index is not applicable for raw barramundi. No GI value is assigned. Glycemic load per serving is 0.0 by definition.

hypertension.key_factor: Low sodium (56 mg/100g) with high potassium (345 mg/100g) yielding a favourable Na:K ratio; omega-3 fatty acids (EPA/DHA) in the fat fraction support endothelial function and modest blood pressure reduction.
hypertension.guidance: Raw barramundi is an excellent protein choice for hypertensive individuals; prepare by steaming or baking without added salt, soy sauce, or oyster sauce to preserve its naturally low sodium profile and cardiovascular benefit.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Zero carbohydrate and zero sugar content; high protein (19.2 g/100g) slows gastric emptying and attenuates post-prandial glucose excursions, making barramundi a glycaemically neutral protein source.
type2_diabetes.guidance: Barramundi is suitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes as a lean, zero-carbohydrate protein; avoid battered, deep-fried, or sweet-glazed preparations that add refined carbohydrates and excess calories.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Moderate total fat (5.3 g/100g) with a favourable unsaturated-to-saturated ratio; omega-3 fatty acids (EPA/DHA) reduce triglycerides, inhibit platelet aggregation, and support anti-inflammatory pathways relevant to atherosclerosis prevention.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Barramundi is a heart-healthy fish recommended 2–3 times per week for cardiovascular risk reduction; steaming or grilling preserves omega-3 content and avoids the addition of pro-inflammatory trans or saturated fats from deep-frying.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Omega-3 fatty acids (EPA/DHA) inhibit pro-inflammatory cytokines (TNF-alpha, IL-6) and reduce synovial inflammation; low saturated fat (1.2 g/100g) and high protein support joint tissue maintenance.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Barramundi is a beneficial anti-inflammatory protein source for rheumatoid arthritis; prefer steaming or baking over deep-frying to preserve omega-3 content and minimise advanced glycation end-product (AGE) formation.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Asian sea bass, commonly known as barramundi (Lates calcarifer), is a white-fleshed, lean saltwater fish widely consumed across Singapore and Southeast Asia. In its raw state, barramundi presents as boneless fillets with firm, moist flesh, a mild and slightly sweet flavour, and a delicate flaky texture when cooked. It is one of the most popular food fish in Singapore, sold fresh in wet markets and supermarkets, and is commonly prepared by steaming with ginger and soy sauce, pan-frying, baking, or grilling. Nutritionally, raw barramundi provides 125 kcal per 100 g, with 19.2 g of high-quality complete protein, 5.3 g of total fat (including omega-3 fatty acids EPA and DHA), and zero carbohydrate. Its sodium content is low at 56 mg/100g, while potassium is notably high at 345 mg/100g, yielding a favourable sodium-to-potassium ratio beneficial for blood pressure management. Cholesterol is moderate at 86 mg/100g. Clinically, barramundi is suitable for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes (zero carbohydrate, high protein), hypertension (low sodium, high potassium, omega-3 support), and cardiovascular disease (favourable fat profile, omega-3 content). It is gluten-free, dairy-free, and halal. The glycemic index is not applicable due to negligible carbohydrate content.
common_user_queries: Can I eat barramundi if I have high blood pressure?
What fish is good for heart health in Singapore?
meal_context: steamed fish with ginger and soy sauce

confidence_reason: Core macronutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (exact match). Fatty acid breakdown cross-referenced with USDA FoodData Central (FDC ID 175159) for barramundi raw. All values are consistent between sources. Confidence is high.
notes: Carbohydrate_g is naturally zero as barramundi is a pure animal-protein food source containing no carbohydrates. Sugar_g is naturally zero by the same basis. Dietary_fibre_g is naturally zero as fish contains no plant-based fibre. Confirmed zero per HPB Singapore Foodo database and USDA FoodData Central. Trans_fat_g is 0.0 (confirmed negligible in fresh fish per USDA FoodData Central). Saturated_fat_g (1.2 g), monounsaturated_fat_g (1.8 g), and polyunsaturated_fat_g (1.6 g) are estimated from USDA FoodData Central FDC ID 175159 (barramundi, raw) as the HPB record does not report detailed fatty acid breakdown; values are consistent with the total fat of 5.3 g/100g. Vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are set to -1: detailed micronutrient and moisture data for this specific HPB barramundi entry are not fully reported in the HPB Foodo database as of 2026; USDA FoodData Central FDC ID 175159 provides partial micronutrient data but was not used to populate these fields to avoid source mixing without explicit cross-validation. Glycemic index is not applicable as this food contains negligible carbohydrates (0.0 g/100g); gi_category set to not_applicable; glycemic_index set to null per critical rule for negligible-carbohydrate foods (not 0). Ontology: FoodOn URI http://purl.obolibrary.org/obo/FOODON_03411433 used as best available match for barramundi; FoodEx2 code A00LC used for sea bass species (Lates calcarifer); SNOMED CT code 226888009 used for barramundi substance. Multilingual names provided for English, Chinese (simplified and traditional), Malay, Tamil, and Hindi as this food is widely consumed in Singapore. All aliases_by_language entries verified to appear verbatim in the flat aliases array. Occasion tag 'snack' from input has been retained in meal_context but is not a primary meal context for raw fish; primary contexts are main meal protein.
